checklist.question: Did receivables grow materially faster than revenue across consecutive periods?
Did non-current 'soft' assets (intangibles, other assets, capitalized items) grow materially faster than the business overall?
Is there a persistent divergence between net income and operating cash flow?
Do inventory levels or gross margins show trends inconsistent with revenue?

evidence.quote: No 'Inventory' concept was provided in the dataset; company reports SalesRevenueServicesNet and CostOfServices consistent with a services business model.

GrossProfit=201602042.32 (2012 annual) / SalesRevenueServicesNet=371234727.66 (2012 annual) = 54.3% margin; GrossProfit=249719456.44 (2013 annual) / SalesRevenueServicesNet=453889907.19 (2013 annual) = 55.0% margin - stable

checklist.question: Is the earnings trajectory unusually smooth relative to the volatility of revenue and cash flow?

evidence.quote: NetIncomeLoss quarterly swings from -4456873.38 (2011 Q3) to 461304.41 (2012 Q1) to 4629862.54 (2012 Q4)

checklist.question: Did liability, allowance, or reserve balances decline (or fail to grow) despite growing business activity?

evidence.quote: AccountsReceivableNetCurrent=60812896.03 (2011-12-31) vs AccountsReceivableNetCurrent=34345555.24 (2010-12-31) -- allowance fell ~58% while receivables grew ~77%
AllowanceForDoubtfulAccountsReceivableCurrent=679943.49 (2013-12-31) remains below the 2010 ratio relative to AccountsReceivableNetCurrent=103968885.07 (2013-12-31)

checklist.question: Does the filing chronology show irregularities (late filings/NT forms, amendments, unusual 8-K frequency)?

evidence.quote: 10-K/A filed 2012-04-26, approximately one month after the original 10-K filed 2012-03-29

checklist.question: Is the provided data sufficient in coverage and length for the above assessments?

evidence.quote: Data spans annual and quarterly XBRL concepts from FY2009 through Q1 2014 (10-Ks filed 2012-03-29, 2013-03-18, 2014-03-17; 10-Qs filed through 2014-05-12), covering revenue, receivables, goodwill/intangibles, cash flow, and equity items.

mechanism_hypotheses.accounting_treatment: Under-provisioning of the allowance for doubtful accounts relative to the rapid growth in gross receivables (allowance fell from 588,643.65 at 2010-12-31 to 245,067.97 at 2011-12-31 while receivables grew from 34,345,555.24 to 60,812,896.03 over the same period, and the allowance/AR ratio remained depressed through 2013 at 0.65% vs 1.71% in 2010). This could understate bad-debt expense and overstate net receivables and net income during the high-growth acquisition period.

Purchase price allocations from a series of acquisitions (goodwill grew from 42,372,732.58 at 2010-12-31 to 158,152,932.77 at 2012-12-31, and combined goodwill+intangibles grew ~69% between 2011 and 2012 vs ~47% revenue growth) may have allocated excessive value to goodwill (non-amortizing) versus identifiable finite-lived intangibles, reducing amortization expense and inflating reported operating income; also raises impairment testing risk if acquired businesses underperform.

affected_line_items: Various balance sheet and disclosure items amended via 10-K/A and 8-K/A filings
mechanism_hypotheses.accounting_treatment: Post-filing amendments (10-K/A filed 2012-04-26 shortly after the original FY2011 10-K, and 8-K/A filings on 2012-03-05 and 2012-10-24 likely related to acquisition accounting) suggest corrections to previously reported figures, which could indicate initial reporting errors requiring later reclassification of acquisition-related assets/liabilities or disclosures.

top_signals: Allowance for doubtful accounts declined sharply (588,643.65 -> 245,067.97) while receivables grew ~77% over the same period (2010-2011), and the allowance/AR ratio never fully recovered to pre-2011 levels through 2013

Goodwill and intangible assets grew substantially faster than revenue during 2010-2012 (goodwill nearly quadrupled from 42.4M to 158.2M while revenue roughly doubled), consistent with an aggressive acquisition roll-up strategy carrying elevated impairment and purchase-accounting risk
Filing chronology includes a 10-K/A shortly after the original FY2011 10-K and two 8-K/A amendments, suggesting corrections to previously reported information

Mitigating factor: operating cash flow consistently and increasingly exceeded net income across all periods (e.g., OCF=51,447,455.36 vs NetIncomeLoss=11,921,835.97 in 2013), which is generally a positive earnings-quality indicator rather than a red flag
